ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Turkish tomato soup; SNOMED CT food coding is limited for prepared ethnic soups.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Turkish tomato soup weighing approximately 300 g, as typically served as a starter at a Turkish restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tomato soup, canned, prepared with water (SR Legacy NDB No. 06160), adapted for creamy Turkish-style preparation with cream and olive oil
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sultan Tomato Soup or Turkish-style creamy tomato soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tomato soup prepared with water (NDB No. 06160, ~35 kcal/100g) was used as the nutritional baseline. Values were adapted upward to reflect the addition of cream and olive oil typical of a Turkish-style preparation, consistent with the input record values (73 kcal, 4.67 g fat, 2.33 g saturated fat per 100 g). Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against the USDA SR Legacy baseline and retained where plausible. Sodium at 250 mg/100 g is consistent with a lightly salted restaurant soup. All values are flagged as estimated adaptations from the USDA approved source.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Tomato soup, canned (GI 38, glucose reference scale)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sultan Tomato Soup or Turkish-style creamy tomato soup. GI of 38 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database, referencing the peer-reviewed publication: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. The entry used is canned tomato soup (GI 38, glucose reference), which is the closest approved analogous food available in the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of cream and olive oil in the Turkish preparation would further lower the effective GI by slowing gastric emptying. GL per serving calculated as: GI (38) × carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 6.0 g/100 g = 18 g) / 100 = 6.84.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 250 mg per 100 g; a 300 g bowl delivers approximately 750 mg sodium, representing approximately 33% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or low-sodium vegetable broth to bring sodium below 200 mg per 100 g, and limit portion to one bowl to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 38) and moderate carbohydrate at 6 g per 100 g; dietary fibre and fat content further blunt postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sultan Tomato Soup is a low-GI option suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes; a standard 300 g bowl delivers approximately 18 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 6.8, which is within a low-GL range, though cream-based saturated fat should be monitored in those with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.33 g per 100 g (7.0 g per 300 g bowl) and sodium at 250 mg per 100 g (750 mg per bowl) are the primary cardiovascular considerations
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, request reduced cream or substitute with low-fat yogurt to lower saturated fat, and request low-sodium broth; the lycopene and antioxidant content of tomatoes provides a cardioprotective offset but does not override the sodium and saturated fat burden at full portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomatoes are a nightshade vegetable containing solanine alkaloids that may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid this dish, as the tomato base contains solanine alkaloids that may aggravate joint inflammation, despite the anti-inflammatory contribution of olive oil and garlic.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sultan Tomato Soup is a Turkish-style creamy tomato soup served as a starter, typically prepared by sautéing onions and garlic in olive oil, adding ripe tomatoes and vegetable broth, simmering until soft, blending to a smooth consistency, and finishing with a swirl of cream and fresh herbs. The result is a velvety, mildly tangy soup with a rich, slightly sweet tomato flavour and a creamy mouthfeel. Per 100 g it provides approximately 73 kcal, 1.8 g protein, 4.7 g fat (2.3 g saturated), 6.0 g carbohydrate, 2.7 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 250 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 38 (low), making it a low-glycaemic option. The dish is halal and vegetarian. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (250 mg/100 g; approximately 750 mg per 300 g bowl) warrants attention for hypertension management, while the low GI and moderate carbohydrate content make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat from cream is a consideration for cardiovascular disease. Tomatoes are nightshade vegetables and may aggravate symptoms in rheumatoid arthritis-sensitive individuals. The dish is commonly served in Turkish and Middle Eastern restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia under the brand name Sultan.
common_user_queries: Is tomato soup good for diabetics?
How many calories in a bowl of tomato soup?
Is tomato soup high in sodium?
Can I eat tomato soup with hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sultan Tomato Soup or Turkish-style creamy tomato soup exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tomato soup entries (an approved secondary global source) and cross-checked against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7 from GrabFood). The adaptation is defensible given the known ingredients and preparation method, but exact restaurant-specific values cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, extended micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Turkish-style creamy tomato soup preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or any other approved source as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.03 g per 100 g from the input record; this trace level is consistent with minor naturally occurring trans fats from dairy cream and is retained as reported. 3. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on canned tomato soup from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283); the cream and olive oil addition in the Turkish preparation would likely lower the effective GI further. 4. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which reflects the restaurant location, not the dish culinary origin; region_of_origin is correctly set to Turkey. 5. This dish is sold under the brand name Sultan via GrabFood Singapore; the canonical food name is Turkish Tomato Soup. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered. 7. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source), adapted for Turkish-style creamy preparation; the input GrabFood source is a forbidden source and was not used as primary evidence — it was used only as a cross-check reference.
